rationale: The cited paper is discussed repeatedly in core theory sections, not just in a background citation list. The citing authors use it to justify key assumptions that number-processing fluency affects liking and that supporting numerical structure can make prices easier to process, and these ideas feed directly into hypotheses H1-H3. In the General Discussion, they explicitly position their contribution as consistent with and building on King and Janiszewski's mechanism, then argue how their results extend it to discount-frame number combinations.
relation_description: Coulter and Roggeveen build on King and Janiszewski's account of fluent number processing and its link to liking by moving from single-number generation or priming effects to multi-number price-promotion offers. They test whether relationships among regular price, sale price, and discounts (approximation sequences and multiples) similarly increase fluency, deal liking, and purchase propensity.
